definition: To study something by breaking it into smaller parts to see how those parts connect to each other.
To judge the quality, importance, or value of a person, situation, or idea.
To question whether a statement, claim, or calculation is true or correct.
To refer to a book, article, or source to show where you got your evidence.
Compare means to look at two or more things to find how they are similar; contrast means to look at two or more things to find how they differ.
To argue in favor of your position or claim, using strong evidence and clear reasoning.
To give a detailed account of something, focusing on its characteristics and features.
The outcome or result of an action, event, or cause.
To judge the quality or worth of an idea, argument, or solution.
To make an idea or situation clear by describing it in more detail or revealing relevant facts.
To clarify an idea by giving a specific example or drawing a picture.
To figure out what is meant by using clues from evidence and what you already know.
To explain the meaning of information presented in various forms, such as text, charts, or maps.
To prove your answer is correct by giving evidence and clear, logical reasons.
A system, diagram, or object that represents a real-world concept or structure.
To make an educated guess about what will happen next or what the outcome will be, based on evidence.
To restate the main points of a text or event in your own, shorter words.
To provide facts, details, or reasons that strengthen an argument or claim.
To take information from different sources and combine it to form a new idea or complete understanding.
The main idea or claim of your essay or paper, which you plan to prove, defend, or explain.
A trend is the general way data or events move or change over time; a pattern is a repeated or recognizable design or sequence.
The event, condition, or reason that makes something happen.
A statement that something is true and that others may agree or disagree with.
Facts, data, or examples used to prove or support a claim.
The clear thinking process that connects evidence to a claim.
A set of claims backed by evidence and reasoning meant to persuade.
A testable prediction or educated guess about how something works.
Information gathered directly through seeing, hearing, measuring, or noting.
A final judgment or statement supported by reasoning and evidence.
A factor or quantity that can change in an experiment or situation.
A standard used for comparison or something kept unchanged in a fair test.
Collected measurements, facts, or observations, often shown in tables or graphs.
Where information comes from, such as a book, website, interview, or document.
A firsthand record from the time of an event, such as a letter, photo, or diary.
An account that interprets or summarizes events after they happened.
To restate someone else's ideas in your own words while keeping the same meaning.
To repeat someone's exact words, usually with citation marks and a source.
Using someone else's words or ideas without giving proper credit.
The surrounding words, situation, or background that helps you understand meaning.
The group of people a piece of writing or speech is meant to reach.
The reason something is created, such as to inform, persuade, or entertain.
The writer's or speaker's attitude toward the topic, shown through word choice.
The central message or big idea that runs through a story or text.
The most important point the author wants readers to understand.
Facts, examples, or explanations that back up the main idea.
Specific words or lines from a reading passage that prove a point.
Something that can be proved true or false with reliable evidence.
A personal belief or judgment that may differ from person to person.
A leaning toward one side that can unfairly influence how information is presented.
A particular point of view shaped by experience, role, or culture.
The angle from which a story is told or an issue is described.
To make a broad statement based on examples or patterns.
To state something clearly and exactly with details.
Clearly stated and directly expressed in the text.
Suggested but not directly stated; you must infer it.
An object, image, or word that stands for a deeper idea.
A figure of speech that compares two unlike things by saying one is the other.
A comparison using "like" or "as" between two different things.
Writing or speech that tells a story with characters and events.
Writing that explains, informs, or clarifies ideas using facts.
Writing or speech meant to convince others to agree or take action.
Notes or marks added to a text to question, summarize, or react.
A plan that lists main ideas and supporting points in order.
An early version of writing used for shaping ideas before polishing.
To improve content, clarity, and organization in a draft.
To fix grammar, spelling, and punctuation in a finished draft.
A word or phrase that connects ideas and shows how sentences relate.
When writing flows logically so readers can follow the ideas easily.
The way words are arranged to form sentences.
A writer's choice of words for tone and meaning.
Words used in non-literal ways to create vivid images or emphasis.
The order of events in time from earliest to latest.
The order in which steps or events occur.
To rank tasks or ideas by importance or urgency.
A group of items that share common traits.
To sort things into groups based on shared characteristics.
To arrange information or objects in a sensible structure.
A careful plan or approach for reaching a goal.
A step-by-step method for doing something correctly.
A neutral, measurable goal or statement not influenced by feelings.
Based on personal feelings or opinions rather than neutral facts.
Directly connected and useful to the topic or question.
Not related to the topic and not helpful for answering the question.
Important or large enough to matter in a discussion or result.
Small or unimportant compared to bigger issues.
Sound and logical when supported by correct reasoning or evidence.
Not logically supported or not acceptable as proof.
Trustworthy because it is consistent and comes from a dependable source.
How believable and trustworthy a source or argument is.
To accept something as true without proof—watch for hidden assumptions.
A starting statement or assumption that reasoning builds upon.
To show something is true using logical steps or strong evidence.
To show a claim is false using counter-evidence or reasoning.
To find an approximate value or answer using rounding or judgment.
Exact and careful with details or measurements.
Close to the exact value but not necessarily exact.
Sensible and matching what you would expect from the evidence.
A relationship where two things tend to change together.
When one thing directly causes another—not just coincidence.
An opposing argument that responds to your main claim.
A response that answers a counterclaim with evidence or reasoning.
A word that limits or softens a claim, such as "often" or "sometimes".
The range or limits of what an argument or study covers.
The way a topic is presented that can highlight certain angles.
A person or group affected by a decision or issue.
A consequence or meaning that follows logically but may not be stated.
Showing subtle differences instead of treating something as all-or-nothing.
To make complex ideas easier without changing core truth unfairly.
To claim something is always true based on too few examples.
An oversimplified belief about a group that ignores individual differences.
Based on observation, measurement, or experiment rather than only theory.
Related to ideas or models that explain how something might work.
Ideas that are general and not tied to one concrete example.
Specific examples or objects you can point to directly.
Having other knowledgeable people check work before publication.
A scoring guide that lists criteria and levels of quality.
Standards used to judge whether something meets expectations.
A box that explains symbols on a map, chart, or graph.
A reference line on a graph showing scale for variables.
The system of marks or units used to measure quantities on a graph.
The equal space between numbered marks on a scale.
How values are spread out across a range or population.
A data point far away from most others in a set.
The middle value when numbers are arranged in order.
The average found by adding values and dividing by how many there are.
The difference between the highest and lowest values in a set.
A smaller group chosen to represent a larger population.
The entire group you want conclusions about.
A selection where every member has an equal chance to be included.
A set of questions used to collect opinions or facts from many people.
A written list of questions used to gather information.
A conversation used to collect detailed answers from a person.
Principles of right and wrong behavior in research and reporting.
Permission given freely after understanding what will happen.
Protecting personal information from unnecessary sharing.
Legal rights controlling copying and use of creative works.
Works anyone may use freely because rights expired or were waived.
A list of sources used for research, usually at the end of a paper.
A note at the bottom of a page that cites or expands on a point.
Extra material at the end of a document with charts or raw data.
An alphabetical list at the end of a book showing page topics.
A list of key terms and definitions, often at the end of a book.
A title that labels a section and shows how ideas are grouped.
A smaller title under a heading that splits a section further.
A sentence that states the main idea of a paragraph.
A sentence that wraps up the paragraph and reinforces the idea.
A sentence that links one paragraph smoothly to the next.
To disprove or weaken an opposing claim with evidence.
To admit a valid point from another side before responding.
To agree that part of an opposing claim is reasonable.
A situation where gaining one benefit means giving up another.
Comparing disadvantages against advantages to decide.
The chance that something unwanted could happen.
Steps taken to reduce harm or likelihood of a bad outcome.
The deepest reason a problem continues to occur.
A visible sign of a problem that may not be the root cause itself.
A weakness or boundary of a study, argument, or model.
Repeating a study or test to check whether results hold.
Whether a tool truly measures what it claims to measure.
Difference between a measured value and the true value.
How closely repeated measurements agree with each other.
How close measurements are to the true or accepted value.
Honest acknowledgment of doubt within a measurement or prediction.
An extra factor that may distort cause-and-effect conclusions.
Placing participants into groups by chance to reduce bias.
To add more detail or explanation to develop an idea fully.
To express ideas clearly and effectively in words.
To explain how two or more things are not the same.
To confirm or support a claim with additional independent evidence.
To back up a statement with strong proof or examples.
To list items one by one in a clear order.
To examine closely and critically for flaws or details.
To combine separate parts into a unified whole.
To form a hypothesis about what might explain observations.
A precise description of how a variable is measured or observed.
A formal reference that identifies a source for borrowed information.
To add explanatory notes to a text or diagram.
To generate many ideas quickly without judging them at first.
What you already know before reading or starting a task.
A mistaken belief that must be corrected with better evidence.
A part compared to a whole, often expressed as a fraction or percent.
A comparison of two quantities, often written with a colon.
A rate out of one hundred used to compare parts to wholes.
A conclusion reached by reasoning from evidence.
Reasoning from general rules to specific cases.
Reasoning from specific observations toward a general rule.
To expect or predict something based on clues or patterns.
To credit something as the cause or source of an outcome.
To make confusing ideas easier to understand.
To gather information from several places into one collection.
To understand fully what a text or speaker means.
To be made up of several parts or members.
To show or describe something using words or images.
To obtain a result by reasoning or calculation from something known.
To invent or plan something carefully after thinking it through.
To recognize something subtle or hard to notice.
Serious speech or writing on a topic, especially in school or formal settings.
To spread information widely to many people.
To record evidence or facts in writing, photos, or files.
A specific area of knowledge, skill, or study.
